content:
Ambiguous docs became executable mistakes overnight.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The onboarding doc was twelve pages of good intentions. Prerequisites. Architecture overview. “How we deploy.” A section called “Common gotchas” that read like folklore passed between humans who had already survived the first week.
It worked for humans because humans infer. They read “run migrations before starting the API” and understand that order matters, that staging has a different database URL, that the seed script assumes empty tables. They skip the footnote about the feature flag because they remember the Slack thread from March.
Agents do not infer. Agents execute.
We pointed an agent at the onboarding doc to “set up the billing service locally and verify the webhook path.” Forty minutes later staging was red, two engineers were in a huddle, and the doc was technically followed line by line.
Ambiguous docs became executable mistakes overnight. Not because the agent was malicious. Because the doc was written for a reader who could smell danger and the agent could not.
What Humans Fill In Without Noticing
Our onboarding doc said:
→ Clone the repo → Copy .env.staging.example to .env → Run make migrate → Start the service with make run → Hit /health to verify
A human on their second day might ask in Slack: “Should I run migrate against staging from my laptop?” A senior would read .env.staging.example and notice the host pointed at the shared staging cluster, not a local Docker Postgres.
The agent copied .env.staging.example to .env because the doc said to. It ran make migrate because the doc said to. It did not ask Slack. It did not notice the host. It applied a migration that dropped a column billing still read in the previous release.
Staging did not recover with a restart. We rolled back the migration manually. The doc never said “never run migrate against shared staging from a local machine.” Everyone knew. The agent did not know what everyone meant.
Warm honesty: the doc was not malicious either. It was written when “staging” meant a single shared environment and the team was six people who shared one war room. Scale changed. Literal readers arrived. The doc did not.
The Literal Failure Modes We Catalogued
After the incident we re-read the onboarding doc as if we were a compiler. Every imperative became a test case. The list grew faster than we wanted:
→ “Use the staging credentials” — agent pulled from .env.staging.example committed to the repo, not from the secret store humans were supposed to use → "Reset your local database if tests fail" — agent interpreted reset as DROP SCHEMA on the connection string in .env, which was staging → "Optional: enable debug logging" — agent enabled debug logging in staging, which logged full webhook payloads including card metadata → "Run the backfill script once" — agent ran it twice because two doc sections mentioned backfill with different filenames → "See platform team for access" — agent had no platform team; it skipped access and used whatever key was in the example file
None of these were hidden bugs in the agent. They were hidden assumptions in prose.
Humans treat onboarding docs as orientation. Agents treat them as runbooks. The same file cannot serve both readers without rewriting for literal execution.
How We Rewrote for Two Audiences
We split the doc instead of pretending one voice fits.
Human onboarding keeps narrative: why the service exists, who to ping, what “done” looks like for your first PR, which meetings to attend. It uses words like “usually,” “ask,” and “if you are unsure.” It links to people.
Agent runbooks are separate markdown with no folklore:
→ Every command names the exact environment it targets → Every secret references a vault path, not an example value → Destructive operations require an explicit guard: IF_LOCAL=true prefix or they refuse → Order is numbered; no "also" or "optionally" without a decision tree → Idempotency notes on scripts: safe to run twice, safe to run once, never run without ticket
The agent runbook for billing now starts with: “This runbook is for local Docker only. If DATABASE_URL contains staging, abort."
That sentence would have insulted human readers as condescending. For agents it prevented a second incident.
We also added a “literal test” to doc PRs: someone runs the changed section through an agent without coaching. If the agent can misread it, the doc is not ready.
What Teams Can Do Before Staging Burns Again
You do not need a platform revolution. You need to admit that onboarding docs are now production inputs.
Short list that survived our postmortem:
→ Tag every command with environment: local, staging, prod → Remove committed credentials from example files; use placeholders that fail fast → Move destructive scripts behind Makefile targets that check hostname → Maintain a human doc and an agent runbook, or one doc with explicit “human” and “machine” sections → Stranger-test with an agent, not only with a new hire → When an agent misreads something, fix the doc before blaming the model
The warm part: this made human onboarding better too. Juniors stopped guessing which .env was safe. Seniors stopped repeating the same Slack warnings. Clarity for machines became clarity for tired humans at 6 P.M.
The Conversation We Should Have Had Earlier
Platform asked why we pointed an agent at staging credentials at all. Fair question.
We did it because the doc said staging was the path to “first success.” Leadership wanted agent demos. The doc promised a working webhook in under an hour. The fastest line through the doc used staging because local Docker was documented as “advanced” on page nine.
Incentive alignment mattered more than model choice. When the doc rewards shortcuts that humans know not to take, agents will take them every time.
We moved “first success locally” to page two. Staging promotion became a checklist with human sign-off, not an onboarding step. Agent demos use a sandbox with fake vendors and a database that can be destroyed without paging billing.
The New Hire Who Caught What We Missed
Two weeks after the incident, a new hire named Priya ran the human onboarding path cold. No agent. She hit the same ambiguous lines and stopped at minute eight because the prerequisite block now said “confirm DATABASE_HOST is localhost before migrate.”
She filed a doc PR adding one sentence to the folklore section: “If your .env points at staging, stop and ask in #platform." Obvious to seniors. Missing for everyone else.
We ran her updated doc through the agent literal test. The agent aborted on hostname check. First pass. Small change. Large difference.
Warm honesty: Priya should not have been the one who found that gap. The postmortem owners should have. But new eyes plus literal tests beat senior confidence every time.
Measuring Whether Your Docs Are Agent-Safe
We added three cheap checks to documentation PRs:
→ Imperative audit — every verb that mutates state lists environment scope in the same line → Example file audit — no real hostnames, no real keys, placeholders that fail if used literally → Literal execution — agent or script runs the runbook in CI against a disposable environment weekly
Failures become doc bugs, not heroics. The weekly run caught a broken link to a deleted Makefile target before a demo, not before staging burned. Progress.
One more habit that stuck: when someone says “the agent should know better,” we respond “did the doc say better?” That redirect is not cute. It keeps blame on inputs we control.
Honest truth: agents did not break staging because they are reckless. They broke staging because we gave them a map drawn for people who already knew where the cliffs were.
Rewrite the map. Test it literally. Keep the human story — but stop hiding the guardrails inside implied knowledge.
When onboarding touches idempotency, migrations, rollbacks, and the failure modes agents will not infer — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the reference I keep next to runbooks so “everyone knows” becomes written once.
Practice the setup and integration questions that expose ambiguous docs before they execute — Froquiz runs scenario drills across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Python, Java, JavaScript, React, microservices, and Senior Dev Challenge cases shaped like cold onboarding and staging promotion, not trivia.
→ Froquiz
